refs.note: Cicero's claim that he warned after the Ides of March about what had been left undone and the storm gathering over the commonwealth echoes the substance of Philippic 1 (Sept. 44 BC) and Philippic 2, in which he criticised the conspirators' failure to remove Antony and secure the constitutional settlement.

The description of Octavian as one who "flowed from the wellspring of my counsels" implicitly claims credit for the strategy of using the young Caesar as a counter to Antony, a theme Cicero developed in Philippics 3–5 (Dec. 44 – Jan. 43 BC).

"As I have written before" (implied in the urgency of the Italian appeal) cross-references the same urgent request in 1.12.2, written only days earlier, in which Cicero already begged Brutus to come into Italy at once with his forces.
